templates.title: PWM Dimmable Light (LEDC)
templates.description: A single-channel dimmable LED or single-color strip driven by an ESP32 LEDC PWM output. Duty-cycle cap keeps a MOSFET-driven strip inside the USB power budget by default.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_pwm_light_id
vars.description: Entity id - MUST be overridden on every extra include (see notes)

vars.name: st_pwm_light_name
vars.description: Entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_pwm_pin
vars.description: GPIO driving the LED/MOSFET gate

vars.name: st_pwm_frequency
vars.description: LEDC PWM frequency (ESP-IDF LEDC default is 1kHz)

vars.name: st_pwm_max_power
vars.description: 0.0-1.0 duty-cycle cap via the output's max_power - THE power limiter

vars.name: st_pwm_restore_mode
vars.description: Boot behaviour (default ALWAYS_OFF to avoid a boot brown-out)

notes: MULTI-INSTANCE - include this file more than once to get several PWM lights. Each extra include MUST override st_pwm_light_id, st_pwm_light_name AND st_pwm_pin; duplicate ids or shared pins are errors. The LEDC output id is derived as ${st_pwm_light_id}_out, so overriding the light id keeps the output id unique too.

POWER CAP - st_pwm_max_power caps the LEDC duty cycle via the output max_power (0.8 = 80% of full brightness). For a single onboard LED this is just headroom, but for a MOSFET-driven single-color strip it is the real current limiter: size the cap to your supply. A short WS2812-equivalent single-color run at ~60 mA/LED will exceed a 500 mA USB budget past ~5-8 LEDs at full duty. Raise toward 1.0 only with an external supply.
LEDC has no per-platform max_power key; max_power is a base float-output option and is the correct place to cap duty.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_pwm_light_id: st_pwm_light
  st_pwm_light_name: "PWM Light"
  st_pwm_frequency: 1000Hz
  st_pwm_max_power: "0.8"
  st_pwm_restore_mode: ALWAYS_OFF

output:
  - platform: ledc
    # id concatenates the light-id var textually, so it stays unique per include.
    id: ${st_pwm_light_id}_out
    pin: ${st_pwm_pin}
    frequency: ${st_pwm_frequency}
    # THE power limiter: caps the PWM duty cycle to keep current in budget.
    max_power: ${st_pwm_max_power}

light:
  - platform: monochromatic
    id: ${st_pwm_light_id}
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_pwm_light_name}"
    output: ${st_pwm_light_id}_out
    restore_mode: ${st_pwm_restore_mode}
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    effects:
      # Slow breathing preset that ramps between dim and bright.
      - pulse:
          name: Slow Pulse
          transition_length: 1s
          update_interval: 2s
          min_brightness: 10%
          max_brightness: 100%
      - strobe:
          name: Strobe
      - flicker:
          name: Flicker
          alpha: 95%
          intensity: 15%

templates.title: RGB Light (3x LEDC PWM)
templates.description: A common-anode/cathode analog RGB light built from three ESP32 LEDC PWM outputs (one per color channel). Per-output duty caps keep it inside the USB power budget by default.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_rgb_light_id
vars.description: Entity id - MUST be overridden on every extra include (see notes)

vars.name: st_rgb_light_name
vars.description: Entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_rgb_pin_r
vars.description: GPIO driving the RED channel

vars.name: st_rgb_pin_g
vars.description: GPIO driving the GREEN channel

vars.name: st_rgb_pin_b
vars.description: GPIO driving the BLUE channel

vars.name: st_rgb_frequency
vars.description: LEDC PWM frequency for all three channels (ESP-IDF LEDC default is 1kHz)

vars.name: st_rgb_max_power
vars.description: 0.0-1.0 duty-cycle cap applied to all three outputs - THE power limiter

vars.name: st_rgb_restore_mode
vars.description: Boot behaviour (default ALWAYS_OFF to avoid a boot brown-out)

notes: MULTI-INSTANCE - include this file more than once to get several RGB lights. Each extra include MUST override st_rgb_light_id, st_rgb_light_name AND all three pins; duplicate ids or shared pins are errors. Output ids are derived as ${st_rgb_light_id}_r/_g/_b, so overriding the light id keeps them unique.

POWER CAP - st_rgb_max_power caps the duty cycle on every channel via the output max_power (0.8 = 80%). With all three channels at full duty a MOSFET-driven RGB strip can pull well past a 500 mA USB budget after only a handful of LEDs (~60 mA/LED at full white). Raise toward 1.0 only with an external supply.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_rgb_light_id: st_rgb_light
  st_rgb_light_name: "RGB Light"
  st_rgb_frequency: 1000Hz
  st_rgb_max_power: "0.8"
  st_rgb_restore_mode: ALWAYS_OFF

output:
  - platform: ledc
    id: ${st_rgb_light_id}_r
    pin: ${st_rgb_pin_r}
    frequency: ${st_rgb_frequency}
    max_power: ${st_rgb_max_power}
  - platform: ledc
    id: ${st_rgb_light_id}_g
    pin: ${st_rgb_pin_g}
    frequency: ${st_rgb_frequency}
    max_power: ${st_rgb_max_power}
  - platform: ledc
    id: ${st_rgb_light_id}_b
    pin: ${st_rgb_pin_b}
    frequency: ${st_rgb_frequency}
    max_power: ${st_rgb_max_power}

light:
  - platform: rgb
    id: ${st_rgb_light_id}
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_rgb_light_name}"
    red: ${st_rgb_light_id}_r
    green: ${st_rgb_light_id}_g
    blue: ${st_rgb_light_id}_b
    restore_mode: ${st_rgb_restore_mode}
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    effects:
      # Slow drift through random colors.
      - random:
          name: Slow Random
          transition_length: 5s
          update_interval: 7s
      - strobe:
          name: Strobe
      - flicker:
          name: Flicker
          alpha: 95%
          intensity: 15%

templates.title: RGBW Light (4x LEDC PWM)
templates.description: An analog RGBW light built from four ESP32 LEDC PWM outputs (R, G, B and a dedicated white channel). Per-output duty caps plus color interlock keep it inside the USB power budget by default.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_rgbw_light_id
vars.description: Entity id - MUST be overridden on every extra include (see notes)

vars.name: st_rgbw_light_name
vars.description: Entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_rgbw_pin_r
vars.description: GPIO driving the RED channel

vars.name: st_rgbw_pin_g
vars.description: GPIO driving the GREEN channel

vars.name: st_rgbw_pin_b
vars.description: GPIO driving the BLUE channel

vars.name: st_rgbw_pin_w
vars.description: GPIO driving the WHITE channel

vars.name: st_rgbw_frequency
vars.description: LEDC PWM frequency for all four channels (ESP-IDF LEDC default is 1kHz)

vars.name: st_rgbw_max_power
vars.description: 0.0-1.0 duty-cycle cap applied to all four outputs - THE power limiter

vars.name: st_rgbw_color_interlock
vars.description: Prevent RGB and W being on at once (halves worst-case current) - C++ literal "true"/"false"

vars.name: st_rgbw_restore_mode
vars.description: Boot behaviour (default ALWAYS_OFF to avoid a boot brown-out)

notes: MULTI-INSTANCE - include this file more than once to get several RGBW lights. Each extra include MUST override st_rgbw_light_id, st_rgbw_light_name AND all four pins; duplicate ids or shared pins are errors. Output ids are derived as ${st_rgbw_light_id}_r/_g/_b/_w, so overriding the light id keeps them unique.

POWER CAP - st_rgbw_max_power caps the duty cycle on every channel via the output max_power (0.8 = 80%). st_rgbw_color_interlock defaults to true: it forbids the RGB and white channels being lit simultaneously, roughly halving the worst-case current draw - an important part of the USB power story. Raise the cap toward 1.0 only with an external supply.
ESPHome's own default for color_interlock is false; this template flips it to true on purpose for power safety. Set it back to "false" if you want RGB+W mixing and have the supply for it.

templates.title: Connectivity Watchdog
templates.description: Config preset that sets the WiFi reboot_timeout, so the device restarts itself if it cannot stay associated to WiFi. Exposes no entities.

vars.name: st_wifi_reboot_timeout
vars.description: How long WiFi may stay disconnected before the device reboots (0s disables the watchdog)

notes: This is built-in ESPHome behavior - the device restarts if WiFi association is lost for the whole window. This file just makes the knob explicit and overridable.
Merge caveat - if the user's own wifi: block also sets reboot_timeout, the main config's value wins over this package.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_wifi_reboot_timeout: 15min

wifi:
  reboot_timeout: ${st_wifi_reboot_timeout}

templates.title: Home Assistant Connected
templates.description: Binary sensor that reports whether the device currently has a live connection to Home Assistant (native API or MQTT).

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

notes: Reports native-API/MQTT connection state (HA reachability), not raw WiFi association - it complements wifi_signal rather than duplicating it.
No update_interval var - the status component polls its connection internally (1s); that cadence is by design and not tunable here.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"

binary_sensor:
  - platform: status
    id: st_ha_status

templates.title: WiFi Channel
templates.description: Reports the primary WiFi channel the device is currently associated on, read directly from the ESP-IDF station driver.

vars.name: st_update_interval
vars.description: How often to read the current channel

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

notes: A channel number is not a measurement, so state_class is intentionally omitted (st_state_class does not apply).

Uses esp_wifi_sta_get_ap_info directly (ESP-IDF). esp_wifi.h is NOT in scope in ESPHome lambdas by default, so this file force-includes it into main.cpp via platformio_options build_src_flags (project sources only - the framework build is untouched).
Returns NAN (state unknown) when the device is not associated.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_update_interval: 60s
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"

esphome:
  # esp_wifi.h is not included in main.cpp by any component, so force-include
  # it for the lambda below. build_src_flags only affects the project's own
  # src/ (main.cpp) and the list concatenates with flags from other templates.
  platformio_options:
    build_src_flags:
      - "-include esp_wifi.h"

sensor:
  - platform: template
    id: st_wifi_channel
    name: "${st_name_prefix}WiFi Channel"
    icon: mdi:wifi-settings
    accuracy_decimals: 0
    entity_category: diagnostic
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    update_interval: ${st_update_interval}
    lambda: |-
      wifi_ap_record_t info;
      if (esp_wifi_sta_get_ap_info(&info) != ESP_OK) return NAN;
      return info.primary;

templates.title: WiFi Disconnect Counter
templates.description: Counts WiFi disconnect events since boot and exposes the running total as a sensor.

vars.name: st_update_interval
vars.description: How often to publish the current disconnect count

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

notes: state_class is hardcoded total_increasing (a counter), so st_state_class does not apply here.
This file's wifi: block merges into the user's wifi config - on_disconnect lists concatenate, so the user's own on_disconnect still runs.
Counts since boot; restore_value is false to avoid NVS wear. For a lifetime count across reboots, `!extend st_wifi_disconnects_value` and set restore_value: true.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_update_interval: 60s
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"

globals:
  - id: st_wifi_disconnects_value
    type: int
    restore_value: false
    initial_value: "0"

wifi:
  on_disconnect:
    then:
      - lambda: 'id(st_wifi_disconnects_value) += 1;'

sensor:
  - platform: template
    id: st_wifi_disconnects
    name: "${st_name_prefix}WiFi Disconnects"
    icon: mdi:wifi-off
    accuracy_decimals: 0
    state_class: total_increasing
    entity_category: diagnostic
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    update_interval: ${st_update_interval}
    lambda: return id(st_wifi_disconnects_value);

notes: wifi_info is event-driven (values refresh on association change), so there is no update_interval to tune.
scan_results is deliberately omitted - it is expensive. Add it with `!extend st_wifi_info_ip` ... or a separate wifi_info block if you need it.
templates.body:

templates.title: WiFi Quality (plain language)
templates.description: Translates RSSI into a plain-language rating (Excellent/Good/Fair/Poor/Very Poor). Carries its own internal RSSI source, so it works standalone.

vars.name: st_update_interval
vars.description: How often to sample RSSI and refresh the rating

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

notes: Includes a hidden wifi_signal sensor (st_wifi_quality_rssi). Including wifi_signal.yaml as well adds a second, independent RSSI read - harmless, but the dBm entity lives there.

templates.title: WiFi Signal Strength
templates.description: Reports WiFi RSSI in dBm and a derived signal-quality percentage. The two entities are coupled - the % is a copy of the dBm reading.

vars.name: st_update_interval
vars.description: How often to sample RSSI

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to every entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entities disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entities from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_state_class
vars.description: state_class for both entities; set "" to opt out of HA long-term statistics

notes: The % is 2*(x+100) clamped to 0..100 - the conventional community approximation, NOT a calibrated RSSI-to-quality mapping.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_update_interval: 60s
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_state_class: measurement

sensor:
  - platform: wifi_signal
    id: st_wifi_signal_dbm
    name: "${st_name_prefix}WiFi Signal dBm"
    device_class: signal_strength
    unit_of_measurement: dBm
    entity_category: diagnostic
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    update_interval: ${st_update_interval}

  # Derived percentage. copy inherits device_class signal_strength from the
  # source, so clear it explicitly - a percentage is not a dBm reading.
  - platform: copy
    source_id: st_wifi_signal_dbm
    id: st_wifi_signal_percent
    name: "${st_name_prefix}WiFi Signal %"
    unit_of_measurement: "%"
    icon: mdi:wifi
    device_class: ""
    entity_category: diagnostic
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    filters:
      - lambda: return min(max(2 * (x + 100.0), 0.0), 100.0);

templates.title: ESP32-CAM Camera (AI-Thinker)
templates.description: esp32_camera preset with the AI-Thinker ESP32-CAM pinout hardcoded. Exposes the onboard OV2640 camera in Home Assistant. Pins are board-specific to the classic AI-Thinker module.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_camera_name
vars.description: Camera entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_camera_resolution
vars.description: Capture resolution (higher = more RAM/slower)

vars.name: st_camera_jpeg_quality
vars.description: JPEG quality, 6 (best) to 63 (worst); lower is higher quality and larger frames

vars.name: st_camera_vertical_flip
vars.description: Flip the image vertically

vars.name: st_camera_horizontal_mirror
vars.description: Mirror the image horizontally

notes: THIS IS THE AI-THINKER PIN MAP ONLY - it is copied from the ESPHome esp32_camera AI-Thinker example and is specific to the classic AI-Thinker ESP32-CAM. Other camera boards (M5, TTGO, ESP32-S3-EYE) use different pins; override the whole pin map via !extend.
esp32 (classic) ONLY - the AI-Thinker board is a WROOM/WROVER classic ESP32. The pin map (and this @platforms line) do not apply to S2/S3/C3/C6 boards.

The camera uses substantial RAM for frame buffers - PSRAM is strongly recommended (the AI-Thinker uses a WROVER module with PSRAM). Add a "psram:" block; without PSRAM you are limited to small resolutions and low quality.
This template hardcodes its own i2c bus (SCCB) on GPIO26/27 with scan:false - it is the camera's dedicated control bus, not the shared core/i2c.yaml bus.
On the AI-Thinker board GPIO0 (external clock) is also the boot/flash-mode strap and GPIO16 is wired to PSRAM - do not repurpose them.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_camera_name: "Camera"
  st_camera_resolution: 800x600
  st_camera_jpeg_quality: "10"
  st_camera_vertical_flip: "false"
  st_camera_horizontal_mirror: "false"

# Dedicated SCCB (I2C) control bus for the OV2640 - AI-Thinker fixed pins.
i2c:
  - id: st_camera_i2c
    sda: GPIO26
    scl: GPIO27
    scan: false

esp32_camera:
  id: st_camera
  name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_camera_name}"
  disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
  internal: ${st_internal}
  # --- AI-Thinker ESP32-CAM fixed pin map (from the esp32_camera docs) ---
  external_clock:
    pin: GPIO0
    frequency: 20MHz
  i2c_id: st_camera_i2c
  data_pins: [GPIO5, GPIO18, GPIO19, GPIO21, GPIO36, GPIO39, GPIO34, GPIO35]
  vsync_pin: GPIO25
  href_pin: GPIO23
  pixel_clock_pin: GPIO22
  power_down_pin: GPIO32
  # --- image settings (user-tunable) ---
  resolution: ${st_camera_resolution}
  jpeg_quality: ${st_camera_jpeg_quality}
  vertical_flip: ${st_camera_vertical_flip}
  horizontal_mirror: ${st_camera_horizontal_mirror}

templates.title: On/Off Fan (GPIO)
templates.description: Simple on/off fan switched through a relay or MOSFET on a GPIO output. No speed control. Multi-instance via id/name vars.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_fan_gpio_id
vars.description: Entity id - MUST be overridden on every extra include (see notes)

vars.name: st_fan_gpio_name
vars.description: Entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_fan_gpio_pin
vars.description: GPIO driving the relay/MOSFET control input

notes: MULTI-INSTANCE - include more than once for several fans. Each extra include MUST override st_fan_gpio_id, st_fan_gpio_name AND st_fan_gpio_pin; duplicate ids or shared pins are errors. The GPIO output id is derived as ${st_fan_gpio_id}_out.

Size the relay/MOSFET for the fan's stall current, not its running current. A DC fan is an inductive load - add a flyback diode across the fan (or use a relay module with one built in) so the back-EMF spike does not destroy the switching device. For a bare MOSFET, use a logic-level part switching the fan's ground.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_fan_gpio_id: st_fan_gpio
  st_fan_gpio_name: "Fan"

output:
  - platform: gpio
    # id concatenates the fan-id var textually, so it stays unique per include.
    id: ${st_fan_gpio_id}_out
    pin: ${st_fan_gpio_pin}

fan:
  - platform: binary
    id: ${st_fan_gpio_id}
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_fan_gpio_name}"
    output: ${st_fan_gpio_id}_out
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}

templates.title: PWM Fan (LEDC)
templates.description: Variable-speed fan driven by an ESP32 LEDC PWM output - a 4-wire PC fan (PWM on the control wire) or a MOSFET-driven 2-wire DC fan. Multi-instance via id/name vars.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_fan_id
vars.description: Entity id - MUST be overridden on every extra include (see notes)

vars.name: st_fan_name
vars.description: Entity name (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_fan_pin
vars.description: GPIO driving the PWM control signal (4-wire fan blue wire, or a MOSFET gate)

vars.name: st_fan_frequency
vars.description: LEDC PWM frequency - 25 kHz is the Intel 4-wire PC-fan standard (keeps PWM out of the audible range)

vars.name: st_fan_speed_count
vars.description: Number of discrete speed steps HA/web exposes

notes: MULTI-INSTANCE - include more than once for several fans. Each extra include MUST override st_fan_id, st_fan_name AND st_fan_pin; duplicate ids or shared pins are errors. The LEDC output id is derived as ${st_fan_id}_out, so overriding the fan id keeps the output id unique too.

4-WIRE fans take the 25 kHz PWM on the control wire directly - the fan is powered from 12 V separately and only the PWM/tach signals touch the ESP. 2-WIRE / 3-WIRE DC fans have no PWM input: switch their power through a logic-level MOSFET on the control pin instead (they will whine at 25 kHz - drop st_fan_frequency to a lower value for those).
TACHOMETER - a 4-wire fan's tach (green) wire gives RPM. Not shipped (needs the fan's pulses-per-revolution), add via !extend:
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_fan_id: st_fan_pwm
  st_fan_name: "Fan"
  st_fan_frequency: 25000Hz
  st_fan_speed_count: "100"

output:
  - platform: ledc
    # id concatenates the fan-id var textually, so it stays unique per include.
    id: ${st_fan_id}_out
    pin: ${st_fan_pin}
    frequency: ${st_fan_frequency}

fan:
  - platform: speed
    id: ${st_fan_id}
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_fan_name}"
    output: ${st_fan_id}_out
    speed_count: ${st_fan_speed_count}
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}

templates.title: GPS Module (NEO-6M)
templates.description: NMEA GPS module (u-blox NEO-6M class) on a dedicated UART. Exposes latitude, longitude, altitude, speed, course and satellite-count sensors, plus a GPS time source.

vars.name: st_update_interval
vars.description: How often the GPS sensors publish

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to every entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entities disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entities from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_state_class
vars.description: state_class for the numeric sensors (set "" to opt out of HA long-term statistics)

vars.name: st_gps_rx_pin
vars.description: GPIO wired to the GPS module's TX pin (the ESP RX). This is the only pin GPS needs.

notes: The GPS module only TRANSMITS NMEA, so the UART needs just an rx_pin (module TX -> ESP RX) at 9600 baud. If you need to send config commands (e.g. change baud/rate), add a tx_pin to st_gps_uart via !extend.
ESP32 has 3 hardware UARTs (C3/C6: 2, one often taken by the logger). This template claims one for the GPS; keep it off the logger's UART pins.

This ships a "time: - platform: gps" source (id st_time_gps). It does NOT collide with core/time_sntp.yaml or time_homeassistant.yaml (different platforms) but they are separate time sources - avoid including several unless you intend it. GPS time only becomes valid once the module has a fix.
Latitude/longitude report NaN and satellites/hdop stay flat until the module gets a fix - a cold start outdoors can take minutes.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_update_interval: 60s
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_state_class: measurement

# Dedicated UART - GPS transmits NMEA to the ESP, so only rx_pin is required.
uart:
  - id: st_gps_uart
    rx_pin: ${st_gps_rx_pin}
    baud_rate: 9600

gps:
  id: st_gps
  uart_id: st_gps_uart
  update_interval: ${st_update_interval}
  latitude:
    id: st_gps_latitude
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Latitude"
    unit_of_measurement: "°"
    accuracy_decimals: 6
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:latitude
  longitude:
    id: st_gps_longitude
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Longitude"
    unit_of_measurement: "°"
    accuracy_decimals: 6
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:longitude
  altitude:
    id: st_gps_altitude
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Altitude"
    unit_of_measurement: "m"
    accuracy_decimals: 1
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:image-filter-hdr
  speed:
    id: st_gps_speed
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Speed"
    unit_of_measurement: "km/h"
    accuracy_decimals: 1
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:speedometer
  course:
    id: st_gps_course
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Course"
    unit_of_measurement: "°"
    accuracy_decimals: 1
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:compass
  satellites:
    id: st_gps_satellites
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS Satellites"
    accuracy_decimals: 0
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:satellite-variant
  hdop:
    id: st_gps_hdop
    name: "${st_name_prefix}GPS HDOP"
    accuracy_decimals: 2
    state_class: "${st_state_class}"
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    icon: mdi:crosshairs-gps

# Separate time platform - valid only after a fix. Coexists with SNTP/HA time.
time:
  - platform: gps
    id: st_time_gps

templates.title: LD2410 mmWave Presence
templates.description: HiLink LD2410 24GHz mmWave radar presence sensor over UART. Exposes occupancy plus moving/still target flags and distances. Owns its own uart: bus.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to every entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entities disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entities from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_state_class
vars.description: state_class for the distance sensors; set "" to opt out of HA long-term statistics

vars.name: st_ld2410_uart_tx_pin
vars.description: GPIO wired to the sensor RX pin (ESP TX)

vars.name: st_ld2410_uart_rx_pin
vars.description: GPIO wired to the sensor TX pin (ESP RX)

notes: The LD2410 uses a fixed 256000 baud UART (parity NONE, 1 stop bit). Use hardware UART pins. ESP32 has 3 hardware UARTs, S2/S3 have 2-3, C3/C6 have 2 - one is usually taken by the logger (UART0), so keep the logger on its default pins or drop its baud/hardware_uart if you run out.

This preset exposes only the core presence readings. The full tuning surface - per-gate move/still sensitivity, detection timeout, bluetooth, engineering mode, calibration buttons - is available by adding the ld2410 number:/select:/switch:/button: platforms with ld2410_id: st_ld2410, or by !extend st_ld2410.

defaults:

    stop_bits: 1

ld2410:
  id: st_ld2410
  uart_id: st_ld2410_uart

binary_sensor:
  - platform: ld2410
    ld2410_id: st_ld2410
    has_target:
      id: st_ld2410_presence
      name: "${st_name_prefix}Presence"
      device_class: occupancy

    has_still_target:

      device_class: occupancy
      disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
      internal: ${st_internal}

sensor:
  - platform: ld2410
    ld2410_id: st_ld2410
    moving_distance:
      id: st_ld2410_moving_distance
      name: "${st_name_prefix}Moving Distance"
      device_class: distance
      unit_of_measurement: cm
      accuracy_decimals: 0
      state_class: "${st_state_class}"
      disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
      internal: ${st_internal}
    still_distance:
      id: st_ld2410_still_distance
      name: "${st_name_prefix}Still Distance"

      accuracy_decimals: 0
      state_class: "${st_state_class}"
      disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}

templates.title: IR Receiver (TSOP)
templates.description: Infrared remote receiver hub for a TSOP-class demodulating IR sensor. Decodes received remote codes and prints them to the log so you can copy them into an ir_transmitter template.

vars.name: st_ir_rx_pin
vars.description: GPIO wired to the TSOP data/OUT pin

vars.name: st_ir_dump
vars.description: Which decoded protocol(s) to print to the log; "all" dumps every known protocol

notes: TSOP-class receivers (TSOP38238 etc.) have an open-collector output that idles HIGH and pulls LOW on a carrier burst, so the pin is configured inverted:true with an internal pull-up. If your module has its own pull-up you can drop the pullup via !extend.

READ CODES FROM THE LOG - point a remote at the receiver and the decoded code prints at INFO level, e.g. "Received NEC: address=0x1234, command=0x0078". Copy those values into ir_transmitter.yaml (st_ir_test_address / st_ir_test_command) to replay the button.
dump also accepts a LIST - to dump several specific protocols instead of one, override via !extend: "remote_receiver: - id: !extend st_ir_receiver / dump: [nec, sony, rc5]".
ADD A BUTTON BINARY_SENSOR via !extend (not shipped, codes are device-specific). Example - a "Power" button on a NEC remote:
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_ir_dump: all

remote_receiver:
  id: st_ir_receiver
  pin:
    number: ${st_ir_rx_pin}
    # TSOP output is active-low and idles high -> invert so bursts read as marks.
    inverted: true
    mode:
      input: true
      pullup: true
  dump: ${st_ir_dump}

templates.title: IR Transmitter (LED)
templates.description: Infrared transmitter hub driving an IR LED, plus a demo template button that sends a sample NEC code. Use ir_receiver to learn codes, then replay them from here.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_ir_tx_pin
vars.description: GPIO driving the IR LED (via a transistor for range)

vars.name: st_ir_tx_id
vars.description: Base id for the demo button (button id is ${st_ir_tx_id}_test)

vars.name: st_ir_tx_name
vars.description: Name of the demo button (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_ir_test_address
vars.description: 16-bit NEC address the demo button transmits

vars.name: st_ir_test_command
vars.description: 16-bit NEC command the demo button transmits

notes: carrier_duty_percent is 50% - the documented value for IR LEDs (the ~38 kHz carrier is on half the time). RF transmitters use 100%; see rf_transmitter.yaml.

The demo button sends a NEC frame via remote_transmitter.transmit_nec. Learn your real codes with ir_receiver.yaml (they print to the log), then set st_ir_test_address / st_ir_test_command, or add more buttons via !extend using transmit_nec / transmit_sony / transmit_raw / transmit_pronto.
An ESP32 GPIO sources only ~20 mA; for usable range drive the IR LED through a transistor/MOSFET rather than directly off the pin.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_ir_tx_id: st_ir_tx
  st_ir_tx_name: "IR Test"
  st_ir_test_address: "0x1234"
  st_ir_test_command: "0x78"

remote_transmitter:
  id: st_ir_transmitter
  pin: ${st_ir_tx_pin}
  # 50% duty is the IR standard (carrier is modulated on/off around ~38 kHz).
  carrier_duty_percent: 50%
  # Explicit: the default flipped to non-blocking in 2025.11 and warns when unset.
  non_blocking: true

button:
  - platform: template
    id: ${st_ir_tx_id}_test
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_ir_tx_name}"
    entity_category: config
    icon: mdi:remote
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    on_press:
      - remote_transmitter.transmit_nec:
          address: ${st_ir_test_address}
          command: ${st_ir_test_command}

templates.title: 433 MHz RF Receiver (RC Switch)
templates.description: 433 MHz RF receiver hub (RXB6 / superheterodyne class) that decodes RC Switch codes from cheap wall plugs and remotes and prints them to the log. Filter/idle/tolerance are pre-tuned for noisy 433 MHz receivers.

vars.name: st_rf_rx_pin
vars.description: GPIO wired to the receiver DATA pin

vars.name: st_rf_dump
vars.description: Which decoded protocol(s) to print to the log

vars.name: st_rf_tolerance
vars.description: Timing tolerance - 60% is the doc-recommended value for noisy 433 MHz receivers

vars.name: st_rf_filter
vars.description: Ignore pulses shorter than this (de-noises the cheap AGC front-end)

vars.name: st_rf_idle
vars.description: Gap that marks the end of a transmission

notes: The filter 4us / idle 4ms / tolerance 60% values are the ESPHome-documented rc_switch tuning for noisy 433 MHz receivers; leave them unless you have a clean signal.

READ CODES FROM THE LOG - press a 433 MHz remote near the receiver and the decoded code prints at INFO, e.g. "Received RCSwitch Raw: protocol=1 data=0x151151". Copy the binary code and protocol into rf_transmitter.yaml to replay it.
ADD A REMOTE BUTTON via !extend (not shipped, codes are device-specific). Example:
Cheap 433 MHz receivers are noisy; a proper antenna (17.3 cm wire) and a superheterodyne module (RXB6) decode far more reliably than the tiny green boards.

templates.title: 433 MHz RF Transmitter (RC Switch)
templates.description: 433 MHz RF transmitter hub (FS1000A / ASK class) plus a demo template button that sends a sample RC Switch code. Learn codes with rf_receiver, then replay them from here.

vars.name: st_name_prefix
vars.description: Prefix prepended to the entity name

vars.name: st_disabled_by_default
vars.description: Ship the entity disabled in HA

vars.name: st_internal
vars.description: Hide the entity from HA/web entirely

vars.name: st_rf_tx_pin
vars.description: GPIO driving the transmitter DATA pin

vars.name: st_rf_tx_id
vars.description: Base id for the demo button (button id is ${st_rf_tx_id}_test)

vars.name: st_rf_tx_name
vars.description: Name of the demo button (after st_name_prefix)

vars.name: st_rf_test_code
vars.description: RC Switch binary code string the demo button transmits

vars.name: st_rf_test_protocol
vars.description: RC Switch protocol number for the demo code

notes: carrier_duty_percent is 100% - the documented value for RF. 433 MHz ASK transmitters have NO subcarrier, so the output must stay on for the whole mark (an IR-style 50% duty would halve the signal). IR transmitters use 50%; see ir_transmitter.yaml.

The demo button sends an RC Switch raw code via remote_transmitter.transmit_rc_switch_raw (code + protocol). Learn your real codes with rf_receiver.yaml (they print to the log), then set st_rf_test_code / st_rf_test_protocol, or add buttons via !extend using transmit_rc_switch_a/_b/_c/_d for typed dip-switch/button remotes.
The bare FS1000A needs a 17.3 cm wire antenna and ideally >3.3 V on VCC for real range; many run it off 5 V with the DATA pin still driven from a 3.3 V GPIO.
templates.body:
defaults:
  st_name_prefix: ""
  st_disabled_by_default: "false"
  st_internal: "false"
  st_rf_tx_id: st_rf_tx
  st_rf_tx_name: "RF Test"
  st_rf_test_code: "000000000001010100010001"
  st_rf_test_protocol: "1"

remote_transmitter:
  id: st_rf_transmitter
  pin: ${st_rf_tx_pin}
  # 100% duty for RF: ASK/OOK transmitters carry no subcarrier, the mark must stay fully on.
  carrier_duty_percent: 100%
  # Explicit: the default flipped to non-blocking in 2025.11 and warns when unset.
  non_blocking: true

button:
  - platform: template
    id: ${st_rf_tx_id}_test
    name: "${st_name_prefix}${st_rf_tx_name}"
    entity_category: config
    icon: mdi:remote
    disabled_by_default: ${st_disabled_by_default}
    internal: ${st_internal}
    on_press:
      - remote_transmitter.transmit_rc_switch_raw:
          code: ${st_rf_test_code}
          protocol: ${st_rf_test_protocol}
